next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 4

By ten the brick had stopped radiating and started merely holding the day's heat. The apartment still felt used up by it. The fan in the window pushed hot air across Lena's desk instead of out, clicking every ninth turn. She had showered anyway because there was only so much of other people's sauce and the walk home and her own skin she could stand carrying. Now the damp ends of her hair soaked the back of an old T-shirt while she stood barefoot in the strip of space between the bed and the stacked storage bins, looking at herself in the black phone screen before it lit.

She knew her angles in the divorced-woman way that had nothing to do with vanity and everything to do with damage control. Chin tipped. Window light if she could steal it. Nothing from below. Nothing full-length unless the dress had structure enough to lie for her. Her Hinge pictures gave Eli a good face, good collarbones, one wedding-guest dress from two years ago that still suggested a waist if you caught it from the right side. They did not give him the soft lower belly that folded when she sat, or the pale pressure marks a bra left under her breasts, or the little scar on her left knee that looked harsher on camera than it did in life. They did not give him the body the last year of marriage had turned into a lighting problem.

Signal lit up with his name.

You home?

Then, before she could answer:

Call me when you're alone.

And then, because he had a good sense for exactly how much to push:

I've been thinking about you since your brother made me say grace over speakerphone.

She smiled...
open_hooks_to_carry: The chapter establishes Gabe's crowded, embodied family world as a baseline Lena both loves and misses.
June arrives late, fixes a broken fan, and folds seamlessly into domestic labor.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...chicken pot pies at Acme were edible or just structurally interesting. The trumpet woman behind his apartment and the kid on Lena's block who kept practicing wheelies badly enough that every attempt ended with a metallic slam and an adult swearing. He asked her to put the phone near the window for a second so he could hear the buses and scooters. She did. He said he liked cities best at the hour when everybody who meant trouble was already either committed to it or asleep.

When he yawned she heard it all the way through, not polite, jaw-cracking, body-first. It made her tenderness for him feel indecently domestic.

"Go to bed," she said.

"You first."

"I'm already in bed."

"Then close your eyes."

"That's too intimate."

"You paused the app."

"You're insufferable."

"And yet."

She did close her eyes. Not because he told her to. Because it let his voice move closer.

When they finally hung up, the room did not widen back out the way it should have. It stayed shaped around him. The rush transcript was still open on her laptop, untouched for an hour. The IRS notice was still under the cable on her desk. The fan still clicked every ninth turn. Her life had not improved in any durable respectable sense. It had simply reorganized itself, almost invisibly, around one person's availability.

She picked the phone back up, turned the volume to loud, and opened her alarm app.

He had said he would be awake before call time and probably free for ten minutes around seven.

She set the alarm twenty minutes earlier than work required and left the phone on the pillow beside her head.
state_deltas_required: family scrutiny, neighborhood intimacy, and Lena's wish to make Eli real in public
Eli crosses from private infatuation into Lena's family and neighborhood life.
